questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When aqueous solutions of \(\mathrm{AgNO_3}\) and \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) are mixed, a white precipitate of \(\mathrm{AgCl}\) forms. What type of reaction is this?
questions.hint: Two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds, one insoluble.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{AgNO_3 + NaCl \rightarrow AgCl + NaNO_3}\) is a double displacement reaction: Ag and Na exchange anions, forming a precipitate (AgCl). Option C would require a free element displacing another, which is not the case here.

source_trace.content_anchor: AgNO_3 + NaCl \u2192 AgCl + NaNO_3. Soluble + Soluble \u2192 Insoluble + Soluble

explanations_tiered.definition: A double displacement reaction involves the exchange of ions between two compounds to form two new compounds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Double displacement (metathesis) reactions occur when cations and anions swap partners. A precipitate, gas, or weak electrolyte formation drives the reaction forward.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two couples swap dance partners: AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB. Here, silver and sodium trade chloride and nitrate ions.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In \(\mathrm{AgNO_3 + NaCl \rightarrow AgCl + NaNO_3}\), Ag\u207a pairs with Cl\u207b to form insoluble AgCl (white precipitate), while Na\u207a pairs with NO\u2083\u207b to form soluble NaNO\u2083.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The net ionic equation is \(\mathrm{Ag^+ + Cl^- \rightarrow AgCl(s)}\). Spectator ions (Na\u207a and NO\u2083\u207b) do not participate. Precipitation occurs because AgCl has a very low K_sp (1.8 \u00d7 10\u207b\u00b9\u2070).
reasoning_steps: Identify two compound reactants (AgNO\u2083 and NaCl)
Identify two compound products (AgCl and NaNO\u2083)
Check that ions have exchanged (Ag\u207a with Cl\u207b, Na\u207a with NO\u2083\u207b)
Classify as double displacement
misconceptions: All double displacement reactions produce a precipitate is incorrect; some produce gases (e.g., \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3 + 2HCl \rightarrow 2NaCl + H_2O + CO_2}\)).
The precipitate is NaNO\u2083 is incorrect; NaNO\u2083 is soluble; AgCl is the precipitate.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Double displacement: AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB. Precipitate forms if product insoluble.

glossary_entry.definition: A metathesis reaction where two soluble compounds form an insoluble precipitate.

flashcard.front: What type of reaction is \(\mathrm{AgNO_3 + NaCl \rightarrow AgCl + NaNO_3}\)?
flashcard.back: A double displacement (metathesis) reaction where ions exchange partners and a precipitate forms.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What reaction type produces a precipitate when AgNO\u2083 and NaCl are mixed?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction in which two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB. Precipitate if product insoluble.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Classic demonstration: mix colorless AgNO\u2083 and NaCl to form white AgCl precipitate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify double displacement reactions and predict precipitates
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. The exchange of ions between two compounds makes this a double displacement reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
questions.subsection: 1.5.3 Chemical Formulas - Empirical and Molecular Formulas
questions.question: What is the empirical formula of a compound that contains \(40.0\%\) C, \(6.7\%\) H, and \(53.3\%\) O?
questions.hint: Convert percentages to moles using atomic masses (C=12, H=1, O=16), then divide by the smallest mole value.
questions.explanation: Moles: C = 40.0/12 = 3.33, H = 6.7/1 = 6.7, O = 53.3/16 = 3.33. Dividing by 3.33 gives C:1, H:2, O:1, so empirical formula \(\mathrm{CH_2O}\). Option B is the molecular formula of acetic acid, C is glucose, D would be \(\mathrm{CHO}\) (not whole-number ratio from these percentages).

calculation_steps: Assume 100 g of compound: C = 40.0 g, H = 6.7 g, O = 53.3 g
Convert to moles: C = 40.0 g / 12 g/mol = 3.33 mol, H = 6.7 g / 1 g/mol = 6.7 mol, O = 53.3 g / 16 g/mol = 3.33 mol
Divide by smallest (3.33): C = 1, H = 2, O = 1

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.3 Chemical Formulas - Empirical and Molecular Formulas
source_trace.content_anchor: Percent composition by mass; empirical formula from percent composition

explanations_tiered.definition: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To find empirical formula from percent composition, convert percentages to mass (assuming 100 g), then to moles, divide by the smallest mole value, and round to nearest whole number.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you have 100 atoms total, count how many are C, H, and O. Here, for every 1 carbon, you have 2 hydrogens and 1 oxygen \u2014 like sugar's smallest building block.
explanations_tiered.advanced: CH\u2082O is the empirical formula for several compounds including formaldehyde (30.03 g/mol), acetic acid (60.06 g/mol, C\u2082H\u2084O\u2082), and glucose (180.16 g/mol, C\u2086H\u2081\u2082O\u2086), all of which have the same simplest ratio.
worked_solution: Step 1: Convert percentages to grams (assume 100 g sample): C = 40.0 g, H = 6.7 g, O = 53.3 g
Step 3: Divide by smallest (3.33): C = 1, H = 2.00, O = 1
reasoning_steps: Assume 100 g of compound
Convert % to mass (same number in grams)
Convert mass to moles using atomic masses
Divide all mole values by the smallest value
If not whole numbers, multiply by integer to get whole numbers
misconceptions: Empirical formula and molecular formula are always the same is incorrect; they can differ (e.g., CH\u2082O vs C\u2082H\u2084O\u2082).
Percentages can be used directly as mole ratios is incorrect; you must divide by atomic mass first.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Empirical formula: simplest whole-number ratio. CH\u2082O from 40% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O.

glossary_entry.definition: Finding the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound from percent composition data.

flashcard.front: What is the empirical formula of a compound with 40% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find the empirical formula from 40% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Empirical formula = smallest whole-number ratio of atoms.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Work through multiple examples: H\u2082O (already simplest), H\u2082O\u2082 (empirical formula HO).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine empirical formulas from percent composition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. CH\u2082O is the simplest ratio (C:H:O = 1:2:1) from the given percentages.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When hydrogen peroxide \((\mathrm{H_2O_2})\) decomposes slowly into water and oxygen gas, what causes this decomposition to occur?

options.A: Exposure to light or the presence of a catalyst
options.D: The reaction is spontaneous and requires no energy

questions.hint: Hydrogen peroxide decomposes naturally but faster with light or a catalyst.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide decomposes slowly over time, but light accelerates photodecomposition, and catalysts like \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) greatly speed up the reaction. Option B is incorrect because heat is not required; decomposition occurs at room temperature. Option C is incomplete (light also works).

source_trace.content_anchor: A decomposition reaction can be carried out using heat (thermal decomposition), light (photo decomposition), electricity (electrical decomposition) or a catalyst

explanations_tiered.definition: Hydrogen peroxide decomposes into water and oxygen through a reaction that can be accelerated by light or catalysts.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Decomposition reactions often require energy input. H\u2082O\u2082 can decompose via photodecomposition (light) or catalytic decomposition (e.g., MnO\u2082), breaking the O-O bond.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Hydrogen peroxide is unstable and wants to break apart. Light acts like a key that unlocks the door faster, and a catalyst is like a shortcut path.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction \(2\mathrm{H_2O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O} + \mathrm{O_2}\) is spontaneous but slow. Light provides activation energy (photodecomposition), while \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy (catalysis).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The O-O bond in peroxide (bond energy \(\sim 142\text{ kJ/mol}\)) is relatively weak. UV light provides sufficient energy to break it. Catalysts like \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) adsorb H\u2082O\u2082 and lower the activation energy to about 56 kJ/mol, greatly increasing the rate.
reasoning_steps: Recall that decomposition requires energy input
Identify possible energy sources: heat, light, electricity
Catalysts provide an alternative pathway, not energy
H\u2082O\u2082 decomposes with light or a catalyst
misconceptions: H\u2082O\u2082 only decomposes with a catalyst is incorrect; it decomposes slowly on its own, especially when exposed to light.
Decomposition always requires high heat is incorrect; photodecomposition uses light, and some decompositions are spontaneous.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: H\u2082O\u2082 \u2192 H\u2082O + O\u2082. Decomposes with light (photodecomposition) or catalyst (MnO\u2082).

glossary_entry.definition: The breakdown of H\u2082O\u2082 into H\u2082O and O\u2082, accelerated by light or catalysts.

flashcard.front: What factors accelerate the decomposition of H\u2082O\u2082?
flashcard.back: Light (photodecomposition) and catalysts such as MnO\u2082 accelerate the reaction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can the decomposition of H\u2082O\u2082 be accelerated?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Exposure to light (photodecomposition) or addition of a catalyst like MnO\u2082.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A decomposition reaction caused by light energy.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: H\u2082O\u2082 \u2192 H\u2082O + O\u2082; light or catalyst speeds it up.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate: add MnO\u2082 to H\u2082O\u2082 to see rapid oxygen evolution (effervescence).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify conditions for decomposition reactions including photodecomposition and catalysis
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Light or a catalyst accelerates the decomposition of H\u2082O\u2082.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the oxidation number of manganese in \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\)?
questions.hint: K = +1, O = -2 (each). Solve for Mn using sum = 0 for neutral compound.
questions.explanation: In \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\): K = +1, O = -2 \u00d7 4 = -8. Let Mn = x. Then \(+1 + x - 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x = +7\). Option A is typical for Mn in some compounds, B is incorrect, D exceeds the maximum (+7 for Mn).

explanations_tiered.definition: The oxidation number of an element is the charge it would have if all bonding electrons were assigned to the more electronegative atom.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Use oxidation number rules: Group 1 metals = +1, oxygen usually = -2, sum of oxidation numbers in a neutral compound = 0.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Potassium (K) always has +1, each oxygen is -2. To balance, manganese must be +7 so that (+1) + (+7) + 4(-2) = 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\): K = +1, O = -2 each (\u00d7 4 = -8). Let Mn = x. Equation: \(+1 + x + (-8) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +7\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Manganese in permanganate is in its highest possible oxidation state (+7). The tetrahedral \(\mathrm{MnO_4^-}\) ion has Mn surrounded by four O atoms, each double bond contributing to the +7 state.
worked_solution: Step 1: K is in Group 1, so oxidation number = +1
Step 2: O is usually -2 (no peroxide here), so total from O = 4 \u00d7 (-2) = -8
Step 3: Neutral compound: sum of oxidation numbers = 0
reasoning_steps: Identify K as +1 (Group IA rule)
Identify O as -2 (usual rule, not peroxide)
Sum of oxidation numbers in neutral compound = 0
misconceptions: Oxygen in KMnO\u2084 is -1 (peroxide) is incorrect; permanganate does not contain peroxide bonds.
Manganese oxidation number varies; it's +7 in KMnO\u2084, not +4 or +2.

glossary_entry.definition: Manganese has an oxidation number of +7 in the permanganate ion \(\mathrm{MnO_4^-}\).

flashcard.front: What is the oxidation number of Mn in KMnO\u2084?

concept_map_nodes.target: oxidizing agent due to high Mn oxidation state

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate the oxidation number of Mn in KMnO\u2084.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The \(\mathrm{MnO_4^-}\) ion, where manganese is in the +7 oxidation state.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to oxidizing ability: higher oxidation state often means stronger oxidizing agent.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine oxidation numbers of elements in given formulas
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. +7 is correct: +1 + Mn + 4(-2) = 0 \u2192 Mn = +7.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 1.6.1 Molar Ratios in Balanced Chemical Equation
questions.question: In the balanced equation \(\mathrm{N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3}\), what is the mole ratio of \(\mathrm{H_2}\) to \(\mathrm{NH_3}\)?
questions.hint: The coefficients give the mole ratios directly.
questions.explanation: From the coefficients: 3 mol \(\mathrm{H_2}\) produce 2 mol \(\mathrm{NH_3}\), so the mole ratio \(\mathrm{H_2}:\mathrm{NH_3}\) is 3:2. Option A is for N\u2082 to NH\u2083 (1:2), B is the inverse, D is H\u2082 to N\u2082 (3:1).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.6.1 Molar Ratios in Balanced Chemical Equation
source_trace.content_anchor: N_2 + 3H_2 \u2192 2NH_3 shows 1 mol N_2 + 3 mol H_2 \u2192 2 mol NH_3

explanations_tiered.definition: The mole ratio is the ratio of coefficients of two substances in a balanced chemical equation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Stoichiometric coefficients in a balanced equation represent the relative number of moles of each substance. These ratios allow conversion between amounts of reactants and products.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The big numbers in front (coefficients) tell you how many of each molecule react. For every 3 hydrogen molecules, you make 2 ammonia molecules \u2014 that's a 3:2 ratio.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In \(\mathrm{N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3}\), the coefficient of H\u2082 is 3 and NH\u2083 is 2, so the mole ratio H\u2082:NH\u2083 = 3:2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mole ratios are derived directly from the stoichiometric coefficients: \(\frac{n_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{n_{\mathrm{NH_3}}} = \frac{3}{2}\). This ratio is used in stoichiometric calculations for mass-mass, volume-volume, and mass-volume problems.
reasoning_steps: Locate the coefficient of H\u2082 (3)
Locate the coefficient of NH\u2083 (2)
Write ratio H\u2082:NH\u2083 as 3:2
misconceptions: The ratio is based on subscripts, not coefficients is incorrect; coefficients give mole ratios, subscripts give atom ratios within a molecule.
Mole ratios can be inverted arbitrarily is incorrect; you must keep the ratio consistent with the question (H\u2082:NH\u2083 vs NH\u2083:H\u2082).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Mole ratio = coefficient of substance A / coefficient of substance B.

glossary_entry.definition: The ratio of the coefficients of two substances in a balanced chemical equation.

flashcard.front: In \(\mathrm{N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3}\), what is the mole ratio H\u2082:NH\u2083?
flashcard.back: 3:2 (coefficient of H\u2082 is 3, coefficient of NH\u2083 is 2).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the H\u2082:NH\u2083 mole ratio in \(\mathrm{N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3}\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 3:2 (from the coefficients: 3 mol H\u2082 produce 2 mol NH\u2083).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The ratio of moles of one substance to moles of another in a balanced equation, given by their coefficients.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Mole ratio = coefficient A / coefficient B. H\u2082:NH\u2083 = 3:2.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students practice writing all possible mole ratios from a given balanced equation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Deduce mole ratios from balanced chemical equations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The coefficient of H\u2082 is 3 and NH\u2083 is 2, so the ratio is 3:2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction \(\mathrm{CaO + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3}\), which statement best explains why this is a combination reaction?

options.A: Two compounds combine to form a single compound
options.B: One compound breaks down into two simpler substances
options.C: A metal displaces another metal from its compound
options.D: Two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds

questions.hint: Count the number of reactants and products.
questions.explanation: Combination reactions require two or more reactants forming a single product. Here, CaO and CO\u2082 (two compounds) combine to form one product, CaCO\u2083. Option B describes decomposition, C is single displacement, D is double displacement.

source_trace.content_anchor: CaO + CO_2 \u2192 CaCO_3. Compound + Compound \u2192 Compound

explanations_tiered.definition: A combination reaction occurs when two or more reactants form a single product, regardless of whether the reactants are elements or compounds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Combination reactions are characterized by a decrease in the number of chemical species. The general form is A + B \u2192 AB, where A and B can be elements or compounds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Calcium oxide (quicklime) and carbon dioxide combine like two ingredients that always make one dish: calcium carbonate (limestone).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathrm{CaO + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3}\) has two reactants and one product, fitting the definition of a combination reaction even though both reactants are compounds.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This reaction is also an example of carbonation of lime, used in construction. It is the reverse of the thermal decomposition of limestone: \(\mathrm{CaCO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO + CO_2}\).
reasoning_steps: Count the number of reactants (2: CaO and CO\u2082)
Count the number of products (1: CaCO\u2083)
More than one reactant, exactly one product = combination reaction

formula_analysis.name: Combination of calcium oxide and carbon dioxide

misconceptions: Combination reactions only involve elements is incorrect; compounds can also combine (like CaO + CO\u2082).
The product must be more complex than reactants is generally true but not required for classification.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Combination: A + B \u2192 AB. Two compounds can combine: CaO + CO\u2082 \u2192 CaCO\u2083.

glossary_entry.definition: A combination reaction where two compounds react to form a single compound.

flashcard.front: Why is \(\mathrm{CaO + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3}\) a combination reaction?
flashcard.back: Two reactants (CaO and CO\u2082) combine to form a single product (CaCO\u2083).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of reaction is CaO + CO\u2082 \u2192 CaCO\u2083?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A combination reaction (two compounds forming one product).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction where two or more reactants form a single product.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Two compounds can combine: CaO + CO\u2082 \u2192 CaCO\u2083.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to real-world: limestone formation in caves (CaCO\u2083 precipitation) and cement production (reverse reaction).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify reactions as combination from given examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Two reactants (CaO and CO\u2082) form one product (CaCO\u2083), which defines a combination reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the oxidation number of chlorine in \(\mathrm{NaClO_3}\)?
questions.hint: Na = +1, O = -2 (each). Solve for Cl using sum = 0.
questions.explanation: In \(\mathrm{NaClO_3}\): Na = +1, O = -2 \u00d7 3 = -6. Let Cl = x. Then \(+1 + x - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow x = +5\). Option A is chloride ion, B is hypochlorite (ClO\u207b), C is chlorite (ClO\u2082\u207b), D is chlorate (ClO\u2083\u207b).

explanations_tiered.definition: The oxidation number of chlorine in chlorate (ClO\u2083\u207b) is +5.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In oxyanions, oxygen has oxidation number -2. The sum of oxidation numbers equals the ion charge. For neutral \(\mathrm{NaClO_3}\), Na = +1, so Cl + 3(-2) = -1 (from the chlorate ion) or +1 + Cl -6 = 0 for the compound.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Sodium is +1, each oxygen is -2. Add them up: +1 + (something) + (-2 + -2 + -2) = 0. The 'something' (chlorine) must be +5 to balance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\mathrm{NaClO_3}\): Na = +1, each O = -2 (total -6). Let Cl = x. Equation: \(+1 + x + (-6) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +5\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chlorine has multiple oxidation states: -1 (Cl\u207b), +1 (ClO\u207b hypochlorite), +3 (ClO\u2082\u207b chlorite), +5 (ClO\u2083\u207b chlorate), and +7 (ClO\u2084\u207b perchlorate). The number of oxygen atoms determines the oxidation state.
worked_solution: Step 1: Na is in Group 1, oxidation number = +1
Step 2: O is usually -2, three O atoms total = 3 \u00d7 (-2) = -6
reasoning_steps: Assign Na = +1 (Group IA rule)
Assign O = -2 (usual rule, not peroxide)
Sum of oxidation numbers = 0 for neutral compound
misconceptions: Chlorine always has oxidation number -1 is incorrect; it varies widely (-1 to +7).
Oxygen is always -2 in all compounds is incorrect (peroxides, OF\u2082 are exceptions).

flashcard.front: What is the oxidation number of Cl in NaClO\u2083?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the oxidation number of Cl in NaClO\u2083?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The \(\mathrm{ClO_3^-}\) ion, where chlorine has an oxidation number of +5.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Teach the pattern: ClO\u207b (Cl=+1), ClO\u2082\u207b (Cl=+3), ClO\u2083\u207b (Cl=+5), ClO\u2084\u207b (Cl=+7).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine oxidation numbers of elements in given formulas

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following reactions is an example of a double displacement reaction?
questions.hint: Look for two compounds exchanging ions to form two new compounds.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{BaCl_2 + Na_2SO_4 \rightarrow BaSO_4 + 2NaCl}\) is a double displacement reaction: Ba and Na exchange anions. Option A is single displacement, B is combination, C is decomposition.

explanations_tiered.definition: A double displacement reaction involves two compounds exchanging ions to form two new compounds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Double displacement reactions follow AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB. A precipitate, gas, or water often forms, driving the reaction forward.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Barium chloride and sodium sulfate swap partners: barium grabs sulfate to make a solid (precipitate), while sodium grabs chloride to stay dissolved.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathrm{BaCl_2 + Na_2SO_4 \rightarrow BaSO_4 \downarrow + 2NaCl}\) shows Ba\u00b2\u207a pairing with SO\u2084\u00b2\u207b to form insoluble BaSO\u2084 (white precipitate), and Na\u207a pairing with Cl\u207b to form soluble NaCl.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The net ionic equation is \(\mathrm{Ba^{2+} + SO_4^{2-} \rightarrow BaSO_4(s)}\). Spectator ions (Na\u207a and Cl\u207b) cancel. BaSO\u2084 has an extremely low K_sp (1.1 \u00d7 10\u207b\u00b9\u2070), making precipitation essentially complete.
reasoning_steps: Identify two compound reactants (BaCl\u2082 and Na\u2082SO\u2084)
Identify two compound products (BaSO\u2084 and NaCl)
Check that ions have exchanged (Ba\u00b2\u207a pairs with SO\u2084\u00b2\u207b, Na\u207a pairs with Cl\u207b)
Classify as double displacement

formula_analysis.name: Double displacement precipitation of barium sulfate

misconceptions: All double displacement reactions have a 1:1 coefficient ratio is incorrect; coefficients may vary (e.g., 2 in front of NaCl).
The precipitate is always the first product written is incorrect; any insoluble product can precipitate.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Double displacement: AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB. BaCl\u2082 + Na\u2082SO\u2084 \u2192 BaSO\u2084\u2193 + 2NaCl.

glossary_entry.definition: A reaction where two compounds exchange ions; often produces a precipitate.

flashcard.front: Which reaction type is \(\mathrm{BaCl_2 + Na_2SO_4 \rightarrow BaSO_4 + 2NaCl}\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which type of reaction is BaCl\u2082 + Na\u2082SO\u2084 \u2192 BaSO\u2084 + 2NaCl?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Double displacement (metathesis) reaction forming a precipitate.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A reaction where two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Double displacement: AB + CD \u2192 AD + CB.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Classic demonstration of BaSO\u2084 precipitate: mix clear solutions of BaCl\u2082 and Na\u2082SO\u2084 to form a thick white solid.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify reactions as double displacement from given examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. BaCl\u2082 and Na\u2082SO\u2084 exchange ions to form BaSO\u2084 and NaCl, a double displacement reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction \(\mathrm{Zn + CuSO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + Cu}\), which substance is the reducing agent and why?
questions.hint: The reducing agent is the substance that is oxidized (loses electrons).
questions.explanation: Zn is oxidized from 0 to +2 (loses electrons), so Zn is the reducing agent. Cu in \(\mathrm{CuSO_4}\) is reduced from +2 to 0, so \(\mathrm{CuSO_4}\) is the oxidizing agent. Option A is wrong because Zn is oxidized, not reduced.

source_trace.content_anchor: Zn reduces Cu\u00b2\u207a by donating two electrons. Therefore, Zn is oxidized and is the reducing agent

explanations_tiered.definition: A reducing agent is a substance that loses electrons (is oxidized) and causes the reduction of another substance.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In \(\mathrm{Zn + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + Cu}\), Zn loses electrons (oxidation number 0 \u2192 +2), so Zn is the reducing agent. Cu\u00b2\u207a gains electrons (+2 \u2192 0), so Cu\u00b2\u207a is the oxidizing agent.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of zinc as a 'giver' that hands electrons to copper. The giver (Zn) is the reducing agent, and it gets oxidized in the process.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In this single displacement redox reaction, Zn (0) is oxidized to Zn\u00b2\u207a, losing 2 electrons. Since it loses electrons (is oxidized), Zn is the reducing agent.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Standard reduction potentials: \(\mathrm{Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Zn}\) (\(E^\circ = -0.76\) V), \(\mathrm{Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu}\) (\(E^\circ = +0.34\) V). Zn has a more negative potential, so it spontaneously reduces Cu\u00b2\u207a while being oxidized.
reasoning_steps: Assign oxidation numbers: Zn (0), Cu (+2 in CuSO\u2084)
Zn changes from 0 to +2 (oxidation, loss of electrons)
The substance that is oxidized is the reducing agent
Therefore, Zn is the reducing agent
misconceptions: The reducing agent is the one that is reduced (incorrect; it's the one that is oxidized).
CuSO\u2084 is the reducing agent because it contains copper (incorrect; Cu is reduced, so CuSO\u2084 is the oxidizing agent).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Reducing agent: loses e\u207b (is oxidized). Zn: 0 \u2192 +2 (loses e\u207b) = reducing agent.

glossary_entry.definition: Zinc metal is the reducing agent; it loses electrons and is oxidized to Zn\u00b2\u207a.

flashcard.front: In \(\mathrm{Zn + CuSO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + Cu}\), what is the reducing agent?
flashcard.back: Zn is the reducing agent because it loses electrons (is oxidized from 0 to +2).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: In Zn + CuSO\u2084 \u2192 ZnSO\u2084 + Cu, is Zn the reducing or oxidizing agent?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Reducing agent; Zn is oxidized (loses electrons, oxidation number 0 \u2192 +2).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A substance that loses electrons (is oxidized) and causes reduction of another substance.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Reducing agent: oxidized (loses e\u207b). Zn: 0 \u2192 +2 = reducing agent.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect to activity series: Zn is above Cu, so it displaces Cu and is the reducing agent.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify reducing and oxidizing agents in redox reactions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Zn is oxidized (0 to +2), so it is the reducing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When a student heats copper(II) carbonate, the green powder turns black and a gas is produced that turns limewater milky. Why does the limewater turn milky?

options.A: The black solid is carbon dioxide
options.B: The gas produced is oxygen which reacts with limewater
options.C: The gas produced is carbon dioxide which forms calcium carbonate precipitate
options.D: The heat causes limewater to decompose

questions.hint: Limewater tests for carbon dioxide gas.
questions.explanation: Copper(II) carbonate decomposes to copper(II) oxide (black) and carbon dioxide: \(\mathrm{CuCO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CuO + CO_2}\). CO\u2082 turns limewater milky due to formation of insoluble \(\mathrm{CaCO_3}\) precipitate. Option A is wrong (CuO is black solid), B is wrong (oxygen does not turn limewater milky), D is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: Experiment 1.2: Decomposition of copper(II) carbonate, limewater test for CO_2

explanations_tiered.definition: Limewater (calcium hydroxide solution) turns milky when carbon dioxide gas is bubbled through it due to formation of insoluble calcium carbonate.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Carbon dioxide reacts with limewater to form a white precipitate of calcium carbonate. This is a confirmatory test for CO\u2082 gas, distinguishing it from other gases like oxygen or hydrogen.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Limewater is like a CO\u2082 detector. When CO\u2082 comes in, it grabs calcium and makes chalk (white solid), turning the clear liquid cloudy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The decomposition \(\mathrm{CuCO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CuO + CO_2}\) produces CO\u2082, which then reacts: \(\mathrm{CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 \downarrow + H_2O}\). The white \(\mathrm{CaCO_3}\) precipitate makes limewater milky.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The limewater test is specific for CO\u2082 because carbonic acid (\(\mathrm{H_2CO_3}\)) forms, then deprotonates and reacts with Ca\u00b2\u207a. Continued CO\u2082 bubbling dissolves the precipitate: \(\mathrm{CaCO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Ca(HCO_3)_2}\) (soluble), clearing the solution.
reasoning_steps: Identify the gas produced from CuCO\u2083 decomposition (CO\u2082)
Recall that CO\u2082 reacts with Ca(OH)\u2082 to form CaCO\u2083
CaCO\u2083 is insoluble and appears as a white milky precipitate
misconceptions: Limewater turns milky with any gas (incorrect; only CO\u2082 produces this specific reaction).
The black solid is CO\u2082 (incorrect; CO\u2082 is a colorless gas; the black solid is CuO).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: CuCO\u2083 \u2192 CuO (black) + CO\u2082. CO\u2082 + Ca(OH)\u2082 \u2192 CaCO\u2083\u2193 (milky).

glossary_entry.definition: A test for carbon dioxide gas, where CO\u2082 turns limewater milky due to CaCO\u2083 precipitate formation.

flashcard.front: Why does limewater turn milky when CuCO\u2083 is heated?
flashcard.back: CuCO\u2083 decomposes to CuO and CO\u2082; CO\u2082 reacts with Ca(OH)\u2082 to form insoluble CaCO\u2083 precipitate (milky).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does limewater become milky when CuCO\u2083 is heated?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: CO\u2082 gas from CuCO\u2083 decomposition reacts with Ca(OH)\u2082 to form insoluble white CaCO\u2083 precipitate.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A chemical test where CO\u2082 gas turns calcium hydroxide solution milky due to CaCO\u2083 formation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate: heat CuCO\u2083, bubble gas through limewater, and show the color change from green to black (CuO) and limewater turning milky.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. CO\u2082 gas reacts with limewater to form insoluble CaCO\u2083, which appears milky.
